column5: “The accelerator significantly enhanced our business performance post-program. It helped us refine our value proposition, clarify our social impact metrics, and solidify our revenue model. Since the program concluded, we’ve seen tangible results: almost 80% income  and increase in brand visibility, stronger partnerships with schools and Daraju Brands , and improved internal systems. The mentorship and exposure provided by the accelerator gave us the momentum and confidence to scale, and we now serve more schools and children than before. Currently we have a ₦12 million sponsorship to reach another 50 schools. Remember in 2024 we took hygiene education to 30 schools under Aurelia sponsorship. It wasn’t just a program—it was a launchpad and we are planning to take hygiene education  to more schools in Alimosho where we are seriously becoming a big brand with multiple impacts"

column14: “We primarily reach children from low-income households in underprivileged communities, particularly in Alimosho, Lagos. These children often attend schools with limited access to clean water, hygiene products, and health education. Through our targeted hygiene education programs, we empower these vulnerable children with knowledge and tools to protect their health. Recently, with support from Daraju, who generously donated hygiene packs worth 20 million naira—including high-quality toothpaste, toothbrushes, and soaps—we’ve been able to scale our outreach and ensure that essential hygiene products reach those who need them most.”
column16: “We calculated the number of people reached by combining both direct and indirect beneficiaries. Directly, we reached over 15,000 children through school-based hygiene education and product distribution. Indirectly, we estimate extended household impact by assuming each child shares hygiene messages and products with an average of 3–4 family members. Based on our data, approximately 50% of those impacted within households are women—typically mothers or female caregivers—who engage with the household hygiene packs we provide. This estimate is supported by verbal feedback from schools and families, showing active female involvement in hygiene practices at home.”
column17: Through WASHathon 30 by Aurelia and 50 Schools Germ Blast by Daraju, we reached children from low-income households in 80 underprivileged schools across Alimosho, Lagos. We delivered hygiene education through storytelling, games, and hygiene ambassadors, while deploying Amaka Chukwu Hygiene Buckets to improve handwashing access. Daraju’s donation of hygiene packs worth 20 million naira (toothpaste, brushes, soaps) extended our impact to households. Approximately 60% of indirect beneficiaries were women, mainly mothers and caregivers who used the products and reinforced hygiene habits at home. These initiatives improved hygiene awareness, encouraged behavioral change, and strengthened community health practices among vulnerable groups.
column20: We primarily reach vulnerable and marginalized groups through schoolchildren from low-income households, especially in underserved areas like Alimosho, Lagos. Due to widespread poverty and high inflation in Nigeria, women and children bear the greatest burden. While we may not always reach women directly, we engage them indirectly through their children. The children become powerful messengers—carrying hygiene knowledge and donated products home—where mothers and caregivers are the ones who help implement these practices. This model ensures that even without direct contact, women are still active participants and beneficiaries of our hygiene and health education efforts.
column22:
We reached our target group—children from low-income households—through school-based programs like WASHathon 30 by Aurelia and 50 Schools Germ Blast by Daraju. These initiatives included handwashing workshops, hygiene storytelling, and the distribution of hygiene packs. We also deployed the Amaka Chukwu Hygiene Buckets to schools, ensuring access to clean water for handwashing.

Impact on the Target Group:

Increased Hygiene Awareness: Over 16,000 children gained practical knowledge on handwashing, clean water use, and oral hygiene.

Behavioral Change: Teachers and parents reported improved hygiene practices among students.

Household Impact: Approximately 50% of beneficiaries were women, who were indirectly reached through children bringing hygiene products and practices home, fostering healthier family habits.
column23:
We calculated the number of people reached in this group by combining direct and indirect engagement:

Direct Engagement: We directly engaged 15,000 children through our WASHathon 30 by Aurelia and 50 Schools Germ Blast by Daraju programs. These children participated in handwashing workshops, hygiene games, and classroom education.

Indirect Engagement: We estimate that each child reached in our program likely shares the knowledge and hygiene practices with 3–4 family members, including mothers, fathers, and siblings. Based on feedback from schools, we estimate that 50% of those impacted at home are women—primarily mothers and female caregivers, who are key to reinforcing hygiene practices at home.
column26: We reached vulnerable and marginalized groups, primarily through children from low-income households in underserved schools. Due to the high levels of poverty and inflation in Nigeria, women and children bear the greatest burden, and while we may not always reach women directly, we engage them indirectly through the children. These children carry home hygiene education and products, enabling mothers and caregivers—often the main household influencers—to adopt and reinforce healthy hygiene practices. Our programs also support children with disabilities in participating through inclusive hygiene education activities that cater to their needs.
column28:
We reached our target group—children from low-income households—through school-based programs like WASHathon 30 by Aurelia and 50 Schools Germ Blast by Daraju. We provided handwashing workshops, hygiene education, and distributed hygiene packs. The Amaka Chukwu Hygiene Buckets were also deployed in schools to ensure clean water access.

Impact on the Target Group:

15,000 children gained essential hygiene knowledge, improving their handwashing practices and overall hygiene.

Teachers and caregivers reported positive behavior changes, reinforcing these practices at home.

Women, especially mothers, were indirectly reached, benefiting from their children’s improved hygiene habits.
column29:
Direct Engagement: We directly engaged 15,000 children through our WASHathon 30 by Aurelia and 50 Schools Germ Blast by Daraju programs. These children participated in workshops, hygiene games, and education sessions.

Indirect Engagement: Each child likely shared the hygiene knowledge and practices learned with 3–4 family members. Based on our observations and feedback, we estimate that 50% of the indirect impact was on women, as they are the primary caregivers and play a crucial role in reinforcing hygiene practices at home.


Thus, the total number reached includes the 15,000 children directly engaged and their family members, particularly mothers and female caregivers.
